videos.speakerOrSource: Brodie, Adon, Sam, Lana, Jarad, and AAC examples
videos.notes: Different ways autistic children and young people communicate

transcript.plainText:
Zoe:
Brodie is autistic. Brodie is considered nonverbal, but he does have a few spoken words, so he can say yes and no, and some other simple words, which if you ask yes/no questions, he can answer. And then he uses some sign language.

Zoe:
Drink? Yeah? More? More drink?

Zoe:
So, he'll sign simple signs like drink, and eat, and thank you, and mostly toilet, because that's an immediate sort of things that he needs to say. And then for larger communication, he uses the Proloquo2Go app on his iPad.

Brodie:
Show bags.

Speaker 3:
They know how to use my communication app or text to talk, and assist me to use it. Sometimes they use visuals, or get me to do the tasks practically, or show me YouTubes that communicate the message. What can teachers do to help students who are finding school or finding things difficult? They should ask if they can help, and they should offer options when you are finding things hard. They should know how to use the device I talk with to communicate with me, so that they know what it is like for me. If teachers know how my communication device works, they can help me. The teacher should also teach other students how to use my device, so they can use it with me too, and it will help them understand how I need to communicate. Remember, I am still learning how to use my device too, and communicating with devices is so slow, too slow for me.

Speaker 4:
I think one really big thing that other people expect is they expect that, through facial expressions, you should be able to read what another person is thinking, and what they're kind of going through. But I don't look at other people's faces when I'm talking, and I don't seek that visual feedback. If I'm having a conversation with someone, I'd much rather be looking somewhere else, because if I'm looking at their face, then I'm taking too much information in, and I'm processing all these separate streams of information, so I would much prefer to look away from someone. And by doing that, I miss out on the visual feedback of how someone's actually responding. What I tend to do is, afterwards, after I've had an interaction, I'll go back, I'll rewind and go back over the situation, and then I'll start to kind of piece together maybe how they were responding in preparation for next time. But I won't do it at the time.

Speaker 5:
How does school make you feel?

Harry:
Happy.

Speaker 5:
So, Harry completes his work in a couple of ways. I use a program called Board Maker to help create the visual cues that are in his PODD book into a task card. And so then he will work through his PODD book and his Tobii, either way, to complete his activities there. And we also use a program called Clicker on his iPad, and that's touch screen. And so then he's able to engage with the touch screen.

Rachael:
Harry communicates with us by using his PODD and his Tobii device.

Alana:
And he points to the pictures in his PODD. For example, he normally communicates and he says, "I have a question."

Jonathan:
His Tobii, he presses on the things to make a sentence.

Speaker 5:
It's important for the children to know how to use Harry's PODD, because there's been times when we've had people come into the classroom before that aren't familiar with AAC communication, and the children go over to Harry and help him to communicate with these unfamiliar adults, and make him feel like his voice is heard, which has been really important. The children in the class not only learn how to communicate with Harry, but Harry learns how to communicate with them, and the children are able to realize that there's lots of different ways that people communicate.

Speaker 10:
Additionally, autism fundamentally, as written in the diagnostic manuals, is a communication disorder. And so there's going to be misinterpretation from both sides of the equation. It's not just the autistic person who is going to misinterpret what you've said, you're going to misinterpret what your autistic friend, student, child has said. For example, there was recently an incident where my mother, who is very autistic, was called rude by a colleague of hers, but actually she wasn't rude at all. Rudeness requires an intent that most autistic people don't have. She's just very blunt, and she didn't realize that how she was communicating was coming across in this negative light, when that was not, in any way, her intention.

Speaker 10:
So, when you are having these communication difficulties, as is inevitable, from both sides of the equation, support your autistic person, student, friend to communicate effectively, make sure you're both on the same page, and ensure that you both understand what is going on in the conversation, because it's really easy for one party to misinterpret something else. So, just support the person on the spectrum to communicate effectively, ask questions if you're not sure, and just realize that mistakes are going to happen, but that's okay too.

Speaker 11:
How did it feel that I had to be forced to deploy the quiet hands technique? Or to wanting to making sure that I wasn't going to be a nuisance, or even a inconvenience to teachers or other students at the school that I was enrolled in? Well, to put it very simplistically and delicately, I would put it out there that it was quite disheartening. It was very irritating and annoying. And it also felt as though I was singled out. I was made out to be a troublemaker for the way that I was behaving. And I think it can go to the point of it being bias or ignorance on the part of the teachers, including the ones that I had for all the subjects that I had to do, including those where I had the same teacher every day. And the way that they reacted and responded when I wanted to express a particular emotional impulse, thought, or feeling, they would stand me down, and sternly and firmly tell me off to not do that, to not stim.

Speaker 11:
So, that is just one of the things that no student should ever have to go through, let alone one who is autistic. So, I guess I can just put it there, very obliquely, that it was not a pleasant experience to make it feel like that. For me, stimming was something that needed to be corrected, that needed to be suppressed. And I would feel that autistic students at school today don't have to go through that. They don't need to feel that they have to be like everyone else. They don't have to behave like someone who is neurotypical.

videos.title: Dan Siegels Hand Model of the Brain
slideTitles: Dan Siegel's Hand Model of the Brain / Slide 11
videos.transcriptFile: Session 5 Slide 11 - Dan Siegels Hand Model of the Brain.txt

transcript.file: Session 5 Slide 11 - Dan Siegels Hand Model of the Brain.txt
transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
This explainer video of Dan Siegel's Hand Model of the Brain is useful in supporting our understanding of behaviour and the role of interoceptive awareness.

Speaker 1:
So what is interoceptive awareness? It is the conscious perception of your internal body signals. Let's use a behaviour scale that starts at zero and ends at 100, with zero reflecting a calm and content individual and 100 reflecting out of control behaviour driven by survival instinct. If someone's interoceptive awareness is still developing, they will not notice or understand what their body is telling them. This means they may start to move up the behaviour scale as a result of internal or external contextual factors, but not notice this escalation and not know they need to make decisions to manage this.

Speaker 1:
99 is when someone has big emotions and it's hard to learn, think clearly, or self-regulate. At 99, your emotions are in control of you, and this can easily escalate to 100 when survival instinct takes control. This model helps us understand how different parts of the brain operate when exploring our understanding of behaviour. It is applicable for all people, not just neurodivergent children.

Speaker 1:
The model uses common terms to highlight three areas of the brain, the reptilian brain, in control of self-preserving behaviour patterns, often called the survival instinct. The limbic brain, in control of emotional, social, and motivational behaviours. The thinking cap of the brain, in control of brain function required for learning, thinking, and mindfulness. The areas don't operate independently of each other, but are interrelated.

Speaker 1:
At zero, both mindful awareness and the thinking cap of the brain are connected and individuals can think, learn, and make choices. At 99, big emotions are in control and it's very difficult to think, learn, and make choices. At 100, an individual enters panic mode and their survival instinct takes over, and the capacity to think, learn, and make choices is overridden by the survival instinct. This can present in a number of different ways. Our body and brain chemicals change along with our autonomic nervous system as we move up and down this behaviour scale. When an individual experiences stress, distress, anger, frustration, et cetera, this increases their sympathetic nervous system and they move higher up the scale.

Speaker 1:
When an individual is engaged in their interests, is motivated content and calm, this decreases their sympathetic nervous system and they move lower down the scale. As individuals start to develop interoceptive awareness, they'll begin to feel their emotions before they're too big and take control of their brain functions. Once aware, they can take actions to decrease the intensity of their emotions, or at least prevent them from escalating. This can prevent emotional overload and avoid someone flipping their lid, which activates the survival instinct.

Speaker 1:
Interoception activities are a way to teach individuals how to become aware of their internal body signals and know when to implement strategies to stay within the zero to 98 scale range. Interoception activities are mindful body awareness activities, and mindful awareness can only be activated when the thinking cap of our brain is in charge. If an individual is in the 100 scale range, the priority is to keep them safe. Calming down and moving below 100 can come later once the brain and body is no longer in survival mode.

videos.title: Disability Standards for Education
videos.transcriptFile: Session 6 Slide 4 - Disability Standards for Education.txt

transcript.file: Session 6 Slide 4 - Disability Standards for Education.txt
transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
In 2005, the Australian government created the disability standards for Education, DSE. The DSE sets out the rights of students with disabilities and also describes what education providers are required to do. The DSE states that students with a disability have the right to participate in education programs on the same basis as students without a disability. All students can join the same educational opportunities and choices and education providers need to make reasonable adjustments so that all people can take part in a meaningful education. The DSE addresses five key focus areas. One, enrollment, two, curriculum, three participation, four, support services, and five, harassment and victimization.

Speaker 1:
You should expect these rights in any school in Australia. These standards also apply to students who care for someone with a disability. Remember, if a student's education is impacted, the school should implement some supports. Different cultures may have different views on disability, and there may be different ideas on how to support people with disability. It is important that these views are considered when planning supports. If the DSE is followed, the learning experiences for all students will be improved.

transcript.file: Session 6 Slide 6 - DSE roles and expectations.txt
transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
Welcome to Sir Joseph Banks High School. We're a high school in southwest Sydney with approximately 700 students from many cultural backgrounds. It is important for me to be involved with my learning plan as it acts as a mold for me of how I should learn and adapt to the changes and the teachers, especially since I'm in a wheelchair. It also enforces my rights as a disabled student to get valued education at the same basis of other students.

Speaker 2:
When the school talk to me about my child, they want to know what my child needs to improve his learning, and I realize they are really looking after him. They want to know is my child get involved in everything that he wants to be? So to me, the school support him and makes him more involved in the program, for the school program. I strongly believe that all students have the right to a quality learning experience at school. I'm glad that my child's friends never had different opinion about him. He was never alienated or make fun.

Speaker 3:
We value knowing our students and how they learn, and we want the families to know that as well and to let them know. And of course, if they have any feedback they'd like us to pick up on or any recommendations to cater to their child's needs, we take that on board and we make sure we can deliver that message within our structures at school.

Speaker 1:
The school helps me to learn and participate by giving me the freedom of being myself, especially since I'm in a wheelchair. People think that I'm suffering or can't do stuff in result of me being in a wheelchair. I'm the captain for Sir Joseph Banks, because I would love to be an advocate for students like myself with disabilities to let them know that nothing could stop us, especially our disabilities, that if we put our mindset to it, we can achieve anything.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
I've always had a lot of difficulty with managing different things going on, or trying to focus on something while there's stuff going on around me. In high school, I always struggled focusing during class because I found classrooms really noisy, and I wasn't comfortable being so close to people and stuff, and people having conversations next to me. And often I'd still be trying to figure out what I was supposed to be doing when the teacher would move on to something else because I process things slower than other people. It meant that I just kept falling further and further behind. Sometimes I'd get quite stressed about that and teachers would try to reassure me by being like, "Oh, it's okay. You can catch up later. Don't worry about finishing that now." But then often they either then wouldn't come back, they would forget to come back to help me figure out what was happening. Like the first task I was given.

Speaker 1:
So I never really figured out what I was supposed to be doing. And then the second task would off and build on the first task, and then the third one would build on that. And I just spent class time just feeling really confused, and overwhelmed, and just lost and not understanding why I couldn't keep up with everyone else. That meant that a lot of the time I just couldn't process or really think about what was happening during class. Speaker 1:
Executive functioning. What is it? Executive functions are brain processes that support us to plan, carry out and complete tasks. They help us guide our behavior and achieve our goals. Let's think about cooking. What executive functioning skills does cooking involve? Planning. Planning what to cook. Organizing. Organizing what materials you need. Task initiation. Starting to cook the meal. Time management. Ensuring the meal is cooked in time. Working memory. Remembering what you need to do at each step.

Speaker 1:
Attention. Maintaining attention, even if you're doing something else, like talking on the phone. Self-regulation. Managing your emotions whilst you cook. Persistence, ensuring you keep going and finish what you started. Flexibility. Being able to change plans if needed. Now, let's think about our young people. What executive functions do they need to succeed at school and home? What executive functions do they find challenging? How does this impact them?

Speaker 1:
Would you like to be more involved in the planning of your education needs?

Alice:
I would love to be more involved in the planning of my education, specifically how they center my education around my needs.

Rishi:
Yeah, I'd like to be more involved, because I really know what I need best.

Michael:
With my education, it's important that I actually have a say. Working with the teachers is all good, but if they're just taking over, it can make it hard for me to learn.

Jake:
I think you definitely should be involved in what you learn, because the way that you learn is going to be different to every single person in your classroom.

Speaker 1:
What do you wish your teachers knew about your learning or accessibility needs?

Rishi:
I wish that they understood that I just need to type my work out more because I'm not really the best at handwriting.

Michael:
We need what we need, but we don't need a lot more.

Alice:
I would like the teacher to respond to me if I came to them with a problem, with a lot of understanding. I would like them to be open to communication in order to fix the problem.

What are the qualities of your favorite teachers?

Alice:
The qualities of my favorite teachers are understanding, able to connect with the students well, communicate well with the students.

Jake:

Michael:
A great value for teachers to have is understanding.

Rishi:

Rishi:

videos.matchNote: Manual match: transcript filename uses Session 1 Slide 10 while facilitator guides show Slide 11.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
Identity is inherently important to us all. By having the chance to say, "this is who I am." It's an opportunity to relate yourself to the world we live in. So why do some people identify with autism in different ways? Person first language has been used by many people on the spectrum around the world. The general idea is that you are putting the person before the autism, but more and more people are saying that they are an autistic person.

Speaker 1:
In identity first language, people say that they are an autistic person as they feel that their diagnosis is fundamental to their identity. The main difference is people first language describes what a person has, not what a person is. Identity first language puts the disability first in the phrase and refers to the disability being an integral part of who that person is. As an autism advocate, I identify as an autistic person. To me, autism is a part of my identity. As I have lived with this condition my whole life. It has taken time for me to be accepting of who I am, and now I have reached a place in my life where I accept that my condition is a part of who I am, hence my phrasing, autistic person.

Speaker 1:
For others, their autism journey is different. They may not be happy with their diagnosis or may not want to share it with the world. That is okay, because that is their choice. If they prefer to be known as a person with autism or by the many other terms, that is their prerogative. The most important part of someone living with autism is that they are comfortable with who they are. Due to the framing of the word autism, it is sometimes met with disabling parameters. As people are usually diagnosed after a social mishap. Older depictions of autism and some modern depictions of autism can have one dimensional viewpoints of our condition.

Speaker 1:
At the end of the day, we are the ones who are autistic, and we are the ones who should be able to define ourselves. I feel that autism isn't exactly a disability, but it's the world around us that disable us. I do feel that autism comes with a fantastic variety of strengths, and that's when we tell the world about our condition, we stand tall and proud of who we are. I hope our world becomes more inclusive, giving us a better appreciation for neurodiversity, and that autistic people and non-autistic people will be treated equally and fairly. One of the ways that we can start is by owning who we are. My name's Thomas Kuzma, and I'm autistic and proud.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
The Positive Partnerships Most Likely - Least Likely template can be used to help uncover why behaviors are occurring. Behaviors are something that you can observe and recreate, such as crying, smiling, sitting, throwing books, or kicking, et cetera. Behavior is not a judgment. When we understand the difference between when behavior occurs and when it doesn't, we can work out how to best minimize or maximize that behavior happening. This animation recreates a true situation involving an autistic primary school student who is struggling to transition, resulting in hitting and screaming and continuing to play with Lego instead of transitioning to the mat. In this case, the initial hypothesis was that the student was avoiding writing. Firstly, the parents and teacher noted the behavior and hypothesis at the top of the most likely, least likely template. Next, the most likely contextual factors of people, place, activity and time were thought about in detail.

Speaker 1:
It was agreed that the behavior was most likely to occur when the classroom teacher was present, although it was acknowledged that there was rarely any other adult present in the classroom. The place this behavior occurred was usually at the Lego area when the teacher was asking the student to transition from Lego to writing. The time of day varied, but it was always at the start of a transition to writing. It was thought that the student was probably experiencing big emotions when they're engaging in the hitting and screaming behaviors. In completing the least likely contextual factors, it was agreed that when the student was in any other area of the classroom other than the Lego area, the behavior was unlikely to occur. This meant that they could be by the books, in the construction area or drawing, for example.

Speaker 1:
In fact, when the student was engaged in any activity other than Lego, they were able to transition successfully to writing. The teacher felt that the student's thinking cap of the brain was connected when they were not engaged in the hitting and screaming behavior. The hypothesis shifted as everyone wondered if the student had difficulty transitioning from Lego to writing. At this point, the teacher reflected on the details of what was happening at that point of transitioning between Lego and writing, and came to a realization that the behavior only occurred when the student was being asked to transition when their Lego model was incomplete. Following on from this, the teacher revealed that she had a shelf in the classroom for students to put their finished Lego model on display for a week, but that unfinished models were expected to be put back into the Lego box at transition to another activity.

Speaker 1:
This detail led to the hypothesis that the student wanted to be able to finish their Lego model so that their work on it was not wasted. The teacher decided to make an environmental change to the classroom and add in a shelf for Lego models that were in progress so that students could go back and complete them the next time they had the opportunity. As a result of this change, once the student had been fully introduced to the in-progress shelf, he was able to put his unfinished model on the shelf and transition to the mat for writing without any difficulty. The hitting and screaming ceased and the student was able to join in the writing activity with no delays increasing their learning.

Caroline Stewart:
The highlight for me has been the way we've worked together in the last 12 months in building those partnerships and relationships, in improving our knowledge of students, working with the parents and seeing how that's really impacted on the school, how the children feel more comfortable, how parents feel more supported.

Caroline Stewart:
To me, that's been a real highlight and having the children just happy to come to school and to fit in, to get their work done and just be the best that they can be.

Natalie Burns:
When working with students with disabilities and diverse learners, celebrating the little things and every little milestone is something that is important as a school, as well as for staff, as well as for students and for families.

Kerry Carter:
That whole, "It's our student." He's not my student. He's not just one individual classroom teacher's student. He's our student and we share the load and I think that gives a better outcome.

Greg:
The things that help as a dad, having an active part with the school and the teachers, like trying to come to all the meetings and having a part in drop-off and pick-up, I think helps a lot. Because then I know the staff as well, and I see what's happening with Piper at school, which helps to work out what's happening at home and how the whole lot melds together.

Janelle:
Just because someone's autistic does not mean that they can't achieve great things. It just means they're a little bit quirky and that sometimes you might have to change the environment, just to allow them to really get the most out of the situation.

Wendy:
You've got to have a good sense of humor, you've got to be flexible, and you've just got to go with the flow.

Megan:
Never underestimate them, or their strengths or their abilities. They might be different, but they're not less. Just go with the gut a lot of the time. If you feel like something's not working, then don't be afraid to say, "This is not working. What can we do?" And seeking the advice. And remember, you are your child's voice a lot of the time.

Daniel Sahyoun:
Autism is a major part of who I am, and without it, I wouldn't have achieved or done half the things I would've.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
Sensory processing. We all have a metaphorical cup. Some of us have a big cup. Some of us have a small cup. The size of our cup represents how much sensory information we are comfortable with. The water represents the sensory information, sometimes called sensory input. Those with a big cup need lots of input to fill their cup, whereas those with a small cup only need a little input to fill their cup, and it can be easy for their cup to overflow. We can have a different sized cup for different senses. This means we can be comfortable with more sensory input from some senses than from others. How big is your cup? How big is your young person's cup?

Speaker 1:
Funny how when you can't talk, people think you can't understand. In fact, they don't even invite your opinion. It is like you don't exist or matter.

Speaker 1:
Please don't go too fast. Sometimes I don't get it. Like the concept might be tricky for me, so I need you to explain it a few ways.

Speaker 1:
Please don't make me always sit by myself with a teacher aide at the back of the room. Please let me sit where I want. Please let me sit with a friend each lesson.

Speaker 1:
Teacher aide, please don't hover over me.

Speaker 1:
Please make the teacher aide a male, not a female. Do you know how I feel when you always give me an older mum type female in every class? Oh, I really love it when you give me a male aide, but having an aide can be such a drag as no kid wants to hang out with you when you always have an aide right there beside you.

Speaker 1:
Include me in the class. Ask me a question. Wait for me to answer on my device, or give me a gesture I can use to speed it up. Please ask me.

Speaker 1:
Please pre-teach me or let my aide or parent know what I will need to know or be able to do to be successful next lesson. Let me investigate the new topic in the holidays so I can get into it.

Speaker 1:
Please don't give me work that looks different to everyone else. Try to keep my booklet the same color, et cetera. I know I am different. I know I can't do the work others can, but it really makes me feel bad about myself when that is made obvious to others.

Speaker 1:
Don't assume. Please check with me, like having to write instructions about brushing my teeth in Year 11. Really, the instructions for making biscuits or turning on my Wii game would be so much better.

Speaker 1:
Please let me go to lunch and recess on my own.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
The thing about autistic sensory perception that most people don't understand is that you either dial your senses up to 11 or you mute them completely. And there's not too much in between unless you're at a very average neurotypical level for your sensory perception. So, for me, my really heightened perceptions are noise and touch noise. Noise, I cannot deal with it. I can deal with very medium, consistent noise, like that in a classroom, of low-level chatter from peers, but I could not deal with very loud, very consistent or sharp noise, like concerts, or scraping against cutlery, or sudden bangs. It's something that I am extremely hypersensitive towards and extremely alert about. Additionally, I'm extremely hyper aware of touch. It's something that I find almost repulsive to a level, hugs, I cannot deal with. It's, instead of being a comforting or warm gesture that I assume most people find them to be, for me, it's like, I feel like I've got sandpaper against my skin, it's horrific. Or casual touches that people use when they're talking to me, I find very off-putting.

Speaker 2:
So, I have three very different children, and I think that's what makes our family pretty special, is that when you have multiple autistic children, you realize that there is no one set sensory profile for an autistic child. I think what's really interesting is, it's really deeply interesting that the same child can have a very different sensory profile day by day. So, what might be triggering or upsetting one day will not be on another day, depending on the circumstance, depending on how much sleep they've had, depending on their mood, or what they're doing, the context. So, I think that's really interesting. And I think it's a lot more... I think the other thing about my kids is that they are both sensory seekers and sensory defensive, depending on who they're with and their particular sense. So, I have a little person who makes the biggest noises, that he's very loud, everything is huge and big and enthusiastic.

Speaker 2:
So, he seeks out his own noise, but he doesn't like other people's noises. So, he's very defensive to other people's noises, but when it comes to his own noise, there's a lot of comfort in the noises he makes himself. So, there are those sorts of things that I think are maybe contradictory to start off with, that people think, how on earth can a child who's so loud also hate loud noises? And yet, that's our lived experience. But I think also it's really important to note that it just depends on how the kids are traveling and what mood they're in and the context they're in. So, if you are at school, for instance, lots and lots of children in a closed in classroom, making a noise, is a very different scenario than if you were in an oval, with the same number of children making that noise, because obviously the acoustics are different and those sorts of things.

Speaker 2:
So, I think it's really hard to delineate one sensory profile for my children, and it really depends on context and a lot of other factors. And I think with me, they have a lot less sensory needs than when they're out in, say, the community, where they're feeling less safe. So, as soon as there are other factors at play, their sensory needs are heightened, or whatever it is that is different in the first place, becomes bigger and more exacerbated and more profound than if it was when we're at home together in a safe space.

transcript.plainText:
Judy Endow:
A lot of times I got blamed for bad behavior, because when sensory things bother you, you do something to fix it. Well, an example, I don't know, I told you not to film me like this, but that's what I'm thinking of, as an example. It's like, for me, when I sit on a chair that has arms, I can feel just where I am, and when I sit on a chair without arms, I have to cognitively keep that in mind, where I'm sitting and not to lean too far one way or the other. And so, I compensate by that, by sitting with my legs crossed. And my mom, she'll always say, "Don't sit like a farmer," and she thinks this is how farmers sit. But why I do that is because I won't lean too far. So, it's a solution to the problem of my sensory system, not giving me reliable information.

Judy Endow:
And so, a lot of times the solutions that people employ become problematic behaviors, because even though it's a solution for them, it doesn't work in the context of their environment. A lot of things don't work well in a school setting, and so then it gets talked about like it's a behavior, it's never a solution, it's always a behavior. So, that's one of the things that's hard, is that people assume, they assume bad character and things like that rather than, wow, that kid's figuring out how to make things work.

Mum:
Yeah, there's lots of things. So she'll wear, at the moment, she'll wear headphones.

Judy Endow:
Noise canceling.

Mum:
Noise-canceling headphones, and they just ensure that they're on and in place.

Dad:
Well, actually, then with the headphones, sometimes when she's in the playground, they'll connect them to her iPad so she can play music and hear music through them as well.

Mum:
Yeah. So they've made some changes there. She does have in her classroom a little beanbag and choice of music there. She has had sunglasses from time to time, but we're having a little bit of difficulty comfort-wise, with the headphones and the sunglasses, but that has been one as well. They've been very good at suggesting to just some weighted things, which go through... On her lap, in terms of a cuddly weighted koala, and they go through phases of working and not working. She has a sensory buttons that she often sits with in class and assembly, that she uses. So, I'm sure I haven't mentioned them all, that's just a few.

Dad:
Does she have cards there?

Mum:
She has cards there too. She likes picking and doing maths with the cards, with playing cards. So, they've always got a pack of cards on her. The bathroom, she goes to a different bathroom where it's not so loud, and the lights are off and things like that. So, they've actually made quite a lot of adjustments.

Charlotte:

Georgia:

blocks.text: Fidget toys helps me, well not really to get my work done, it's usually to help me concentrate. We also got chewies made out of rubber so you can chew them. I like fidget spinners because you are able to just sit here and you're able to concentrate.

blocks.speaker: Charlotte
blocks.text: Sometimes if I'm feeling overwhelmed, I like to read a book at my desk, or go to the reading corner, or I like to use my headphones, or tell Ms. Gibbs that I need some time out in the calm room.

blocks.speaker: Kylie Gibbs
blocks.text: The sensory supports that I have in place include tactile support, so Play-Doh, water beads. I have pillows, cushions of different fabrics. I also have auditory supports. So Charlotte particularly requires headphones because my classroom does get very loud.

blocks.speaker: Madison
blocks.text: Having headphones and music helps me, sometimes a fidget cube, and my sequin pencil case I have that helps my sensory.

blocks.speaker: Mykyla Wright
blocks.text: I support Zavier to get his homework done by sitting with him, encouraging him, and letting him take his time.

blocks.speaker: Aiden
blocks.text: Having a reminder and a clear order helps me to take turns at school. So say if it was a whole class and I was playing the game, I was a leader, and I would say it would go me, Xavier, this person, this person, Ethan.

blocks.speaker: Neil Grimes
blocks.text: So his special interest really drives things like asking questions, socializing, seeking information, and problem solving.

blocks.speaker: Kylie Gibbs
blocks.text: My students are supported in class to achieve their learning goals by me breaking down complex task into more manageable and achievable goals. Their lesson goals are clear throughout the lesson.

blocks.speaker: Charlotte
blocks.text: With my writing, I've set a goal to write four sentences or more.

blocks.speaker: Madison
blocks.text: When I'm completing assessment tasks, it helps to have a timeline of each step and when that's supposed to be completed by. I like to learn visually by seeing the thing in front of me. Sometimes listening works as well, it just really depends on the subject. When teaching is done in a different way, that's when learning's really fun. I tend not to like the lessons where you sit down and just listen to the teacher talk the whole lesson, it's those ones where they have maybe YouTube clips or interactive ways to learn. There's a thing called Kahoot which is really fun and engaging.

blocks.speaker: Georgia
blocks.text: Sometimes I like timers and countdowns because they help me to know how long I have left on a certain task, but then other times they stress me out because they put the pressure on.

blocks.speaker: India Lennerth
blocks.text: We use technology a lot, so Google Classroom is a really, really important and effective tool that we use to help our students stay on track and focused on their tasks.

blocks.speaker: Dylan
blocks.text: Being autistic helps me to learn because I can learn in different ways to everyone. It also stops me from learning sometimes because I can't cope in classes because of how loud it is or it's too disruptive. My teachers make learning interesting and fun for me when they try to make all the activities hands-on. When I'm completing assessment tasks, it helps me to have lots of breaks because I can't sit still for very long.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
But I think that I probably could have done actually a lot better if I'd actually applied myself and maybe if I'd just had one of the teachers pull me aside and have a chat about what I wanted to learn about or what I was interested in. Because at the time, I didn't know what I was interested in. I didn't know what we could learn about. I wanted to learn about psychology and the brain, and I even did a psychology class in Year 8, but we didn't learn about the stuff that I felt like I wanted to learn about. I wanted to know how the brain works and how the senses work, but I didn't start learning that stuff until I was in my third year of university doing a neuroscience subject. And I was learning the stuff there, and I thought, "This is what I've been wanting to learn for ages. This is the stuff that is really engaging for me." And I just wish I'd had the opportunity to engage more with it at that time.

Xander:
My name's Xander. I enjoy music, space, history, human body, Minecraft, Titanic, et cetera. Also enjoy eating and Uno, friends, sports, cricket, NRL, and also tennis. Yeah.

Elise:
Instead of pulling me away from my interest, because there's... A lot of people thought I'm in my own little world, all I'm interested is in my sport, which I really was, but that's because that's what I understood, and everything that I learned, I tried to, well, in order to understand it, I needed to fit it in to what I knew, which was my sport. So instead of pulling me away from my interest and seeing that as the hindrance for me to connect, actually going, "How can we leverage this special interest as a motivation for me to succeed in terms of understanding and connecting?" So bringing what the learning is into what the person's great at, that's having a strengths-based approach. If it's about me being time manager, and giving me a whiteboard and saying, "All right, you're going to... Every time..." Say, if I'm struggling with transitions, "All right, Elise, at 10:00, you can tick off this when we move into this next group." And so I've got that movement, that transition. There's so many amazing little, little tricks that would help so I'm not going to be overloaded.

transcript.plainText:
Speaker 1:
I can also see a degree of beauty in things around me that I don't think neurotypical people often see. One example of that is I can see mist rising up from the surface of the earth, and I can see that interacting with light. If you've ever been on a lake early in the morning, you can see the mist rise off the water. Well, I can see that interaction of light and water particles anywhere. One of the things that's important to remember is that not everybody with autism will perceive things exactly the way I do.
